715	// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
716	// Internal: apply side effects that are pure SQLite operations
717	// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
718	
719	function applyPendingSideEffects(
720	  db: SqliteDb,
721	  sideEffects: readonly string[],
722	  workflowId: string,
723	  itemId: string,
724	  stage: string,
725	  phase: string,
726	  now: string,
727	): void {
728	  const kind = pendingAttentionKindFromEffects(sideEffects);
729	  if (kind) {
730	    writePendingAttention(db, workflowId, kind, { item_id: itemId, stage, phase }, now);
731	  }
732	}
733	
734	// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
735	// applyItemTransition — public
736	// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
737	
738	/**
739	 * Atomically apply a state-machine event to an item.
740	 *
741	 * All SQLite mutations (item status, events row, optional pending_attention,
742	 * optional cascade-block) are committed in a single db.transaction() before
743	 * any result is returned to the caller.
744	 *
745	 * The caller is responsible for executing any non-SQLite side effects
746	 * (process spawning, signal sending, WS frame emission) AFTER this function
747	 * returns, using the `sideEffects` labels in the result.
748	 *
749	 * @throws if the item or workflow row is not found.
750	 * @throws if a required guard-context field is missing for a conditional.
751	 */
752	export function applyItemTransition(
753	  params: ApplyItemTransitionParams,
754	): ApplyItemTransitionResult {
755	  return params.db.transaction((db) => {
756	    const now = new Date().toISOString();
757	
758	    // Read current item state — re-read from SQLite every call (RC-1).
759	    const item = db
760	      .prepare('SELECT * FROM items WHERE id = ?')
761	      .get(params.itemId) as ItemRow | undefined;
762	    if (!item) throw new Error(`Item not found: ${params.itemId}`);
763	
764	    // Read workflow for worktree_path guard.
765	    const workflow = db
766	      .prepare(
767	        'SELECT id, status, current_stage, worktree_path, pipeline FROM workflows WHERE id = ?',
768	      )
769	      .get(params.workflowId) as WorkflowRow | undefined;
770	    if (!workflow) throw new Error(`Workflow not found: ${params.workflowId}`);
771	
772	    const currentState = item.status as State;
773	    const ctx = params.guardCtx ?? {};
774	
775	    // Look up transition result.
776	    const transitionResult: TransitionResult | undefined = transition(
777	      currentState,
778	      params.event,
779	    );
780	
781	    // Unknown (state, event) pair → log and return no-op.
782	    if (!transitionResult) {
783	      writeEvent(db, {
784	        ts: now,
785	        workflowId: params.workflowId,
786	        itemId: params.itemId,
787	        sessionId: params.sessionId,
788	        stage: params.stage,
789	        phase: params.phase,
790	        attempt: params.attempt,
791	        eventType: `noop:${params.event}`,
792	        level: 'warn',
793	        message: `No transition for (${currentState}, ${params.event})`,
794	      });
795	      return {
796	        newState: currentState,
797	        newPhase: item.current_phase,
798	        sideEffects: [],
799	        cascadeBlocked: false,
800	        stageComplete: false,
801	      };
802	    }
803	
804	    // Resolve concrete outcome.
805	    let selection: OutcomeSelection;
806	    if (transitionResult.kind === 'direct') {
807	      selection = {
808	        to: transitionResult.to,
809	        sideEffects: transitionResult.sideEffects,
810	        newPhase:
811	          transitionResult.sideEffects.includes('advance current_phase')
812	            ? (ctx.nextPhase ?? item.current_phase ?? undefined)
813	            : undefined,
814	      };
815	    } else {
816	      selection = selectConditionalOutcome(
817	        transitionResult,
818	        currentState,
819	        params.event,
820	        item,
821	        workflow,
822	        db,
823	        ctx,
824	        now,
825	        {
826	          workflowId: params.workflowId,
827	          itemId: params.itemId,
828	          sessionId: params.sessionId,
829	          stage: params.stage,
830	          phase: params.phase,
831	          attempt: params.attempt,
832	        },
833	      );
834	    }
835	
836	    const newState = selection.to;
837	    const newRetryCount =
838	      selection.newRetryCount !== undefined
839	        ? selection.newRetryCount
840	        : item.retry_count;
841	
842	    // Determine new phase.
843	    let newPhase: string | null;
844	    if (selection.newPhase !== undefined) {
845	      newPhase = selection.newPhase;
846	    } else if (newState === 'in_progress' && item.current_phase !== null) {
847	      // Keep current phase unless explicitly advanced.
848	      newPhase = item.current_phase;
849	    } else {
850	      newPhase = item.current_phase;
851	    }
852	
853	    // Blocked reason: set on entering blocked state, preserve on other states.
854	    const newBlockedReason =
855	      newState === 'blocked'
856	        ? (`dependency ${params.itemId} ${currentState}`)
857	        : item.blocked_reason;
858	
859	    // Persist item changes.
860	    db.prepare(`
861	      UPDATE items
862	         SET status         = ?,
863	             current_phase  = ?,
864	             retry_count    = ?,
865	             blocked_reason = ?,
866	             updated_at     = ?
867	       WHERE id = ?
868	    `).run(newState, newPhase, newRetryCount, newBlockedReason, now, params.itemId);
869	
870	    // Write events row for EVERY transition (RC-5, AC-6).
871	    writeEvent(db, {
872	      ts: now,
873	      workflowId: params.workflowId,
874	      itemId: params.itemId,
875	      sessionId: params.sessionId,
876	      stage: params.stage,
877	      phase: params.phase,
878	      attempt: params.attempt,
879	      eventType: params.event,
880	      level: 'info',
881	      message: `${currentState} → ${newState}`,
882	      extra:
883	        selection.retryMode
884	          ? JSON.stringify({ retryMode: selection.retryMode })
885	          : null,
886	    });
887	
888	    // Apply pure-SQLite side effects (pending_attention rows).
889	    applyPendingSideEffects(
890	      db,
891	      selection.sideEffects,
892	      params.workflowId,
893	      params.itemId,
894	      params.stage,
895	      params.phase,
896	      now,
897	    );
898	
899	    // Cascade-block dependents in the same transaction (AC-2).

content.thinking:
Now I have enough context to implement the scheduler. Let me also look at what the `buildCrashRecovery` returns and the `applyWorktreeCreated` function, plus the `server.ts` `listenServer` function to see how I can plug in.

Let me also look at what frame types are available and understand the broadcast mechanism.

Now I have a very good understanding of the codebase. Let me plan the implementation of the scheduler.

## Scheduler Design

The scheduler needs to be a class with injectable dependencies:
- `ProcessManager`
- `WorktreeManager`
- `PromptAssembler`
- `PrePostRunner`

It needs to:
1. On startup: call `buildCrashRecovery` then enter a poll/event loop
2. Poll SQLite for runnable items (items in `ready` state with deps satisfied)
3. For each runnable item:
   - Transition item to `in_progress` via `applyItemTransition` (before spawn)
   - Assemble the prompt, create a worktree if needed, run pre-commands, and spawn the process
   - Parse the stdout stream through JSON and fire item transitions based on classifier events
   - Execute post-commands and broadcast WebSocket frames for each state change
4. Handle rate limiting by transitioning to `rate_limited` and scheduling a retry after the reset time
5. On shutdown signals, gracefully drain pending work and cancel in-flight sessions before exiting

The key architectural choices are reading from SQLite on every scheduling decision rather than caching in memory, routing all database mutations through `applyItemTransition`, and only spawning processes after the `in_progress` transition is committed. The system calls `buildCrashRecovery` at startup, enforces concurrency limits by querying active sessions from the database, and keeps the core components injectable for testing.

For the WebSocket broadcast mechanism, I need to figure out how to push frames to all connected clients. The `ServerState` has `seqStore` and `backfillBuffer` available, but the current ws.ts implementation doesn't expose a broadcast function yet, so I'll need to add that capability. I need to add a client registry to track WebSocket connections and their workflow subscriptions, then expose a broadcast function that the scheduler can call to send state change frames to all connected clients for a given workflow.

Now I'm mapping out the specific files to create and modify—the scheduler class itself, its tests, and updates to the server and API modules to wire everything together.

For the WebSocket broadcast piece, I need to track connected clients and emit state change frames when the scheduler updates items, so I'll add a registry to `ws.ts` and expose the broadcast capability through `server.ts`.

On session tracking, the scheduler will keep in-flight sessions in memory by item ID, but the actual concurrency limit has to come from querying SQLite for running sessions—that's the constraint.

For workflow setup, when `yoke start` runs on a repo with `.yoke.yml`, I need to parse the pipeline config and upsert the workflow and item records into SQLite. For `per-item` stages, items come from the specified file; for `once` stages, I create a single synthetic item.

The startup sequence is: upsert workflow and items, call `buildCrashRecovery`, then begin the scheduling loop—all within 2 seconds. The scheduler then transitions items through states based on dependency satisfaction: `pending` → `deps_satisfied` → `ready` (once all dependencies are complete), then `ready` → `phase_start` → `bootstrapping` when spawning begins.

Looking at the state machine more carefully, I need to handle the branching logic where items either go to `bootstrapping` if they need a worktree created, or directly to `in_progress` if one already exists. The scheduling loop should iterate through items in each state, fire the appropriate events, and handle the bootstrap process before spawning sessions. The scheduler is really the orchestration layer that coordinates everything—it reads from SQLite to find items ready to run, assembles prompts, manages worktrees, spawns sessions, parses the output stream, and fires state transitions based on classifier events. Looking at the dependency logic in AC-2, items start in pending and only move to in_progress once their dependencies are satisfied, which happens before ProcessManager even spawns anything. So each scheduler loop needs to check concurrency limits per workflow, find pending items with met dependencies, and transition them forward. to `ready`
   d. Find items in `ready` state
      - If no worktree_path, create worktree first
      - Then fire `phase_start` → moves to `in_progress` (or `bootstrapping`)
   e. For items in `bootstrapping` → run bootstrap
   f. For items in `in_progress` with no active session → spawn

Actually, wait. Let me look at the original design again. The flow in `docs/architecture.md §5` is:
1. `(ready, phase_start)` → `bootstrapping` → `in_progress`
2. Worktree Manager creates worktree + branch (first phase of first per-item stage only)
3. Build PromptContext + call Prompt Assembler
4. Pre/Post Runner executes `pre:` commands
5. Process Manager `spawn(cmd, args)`, streams stdin buffer, parses stdout NDJSON
6. On child exit, Process Manager raises `session_ok` / `session_fail`
7. Pre/Post Runner executes `post:` commands
8. Artifact validators
9. Diff check
10. If more phases remain, advance to next phase

So the scheduler needs to handle three main transitions: fire `deps_satisfied` when all dependencies complete to move items from pending to ready, then fire `phase_start` to move into bootstrapping or directly into in_progress depending on whether a worktree exists, and finally manage the in_progress state by assembling the prompt, running pre-commands, spawning the session with NDJSON parsing, handling the exit status, running post-commands, validating artifacts, checking diffs, and advancing to the next phase if there are more to process.

Now I'm planning the file structure and defining the Scheduler class with its dependencies and in-flight session tracking.

Defining Scheduler class... Setting up the polling mechanism with a tick interval, where each tick processes all active workflows and respects concurrency limits, then reschedules itself unless the scheduler is stopped.

Now I'm thinking through how `ingestWorkflow` should handle the initial setup when `.yoke.yml` is loaded—for `run: once` stages, there's a single synthetic item per stage, while `run: per-item` stages read items from a manifest file and extract them using the configured `items_list` or `items_id` fields, storing the opaque item data in SQLite's items table alongside the stage reference. , starting with `current_phase = 'plan'`. But I need to check the architecture more carefully—it seems like for `run: once` stages there's implicitly one item, while for `run: per-item` stages I need to read the manifest and create an item for each entry. The data flow suggests the pipeline engine reads workflow and item rows, transitions them through states like ready and in_progress, and the worktree manager only creates a worktree on the first phase of the first per-item stage.

Now I'm seeing how the phases work: for a `run: once` stage with multiple phases, there's a single item advancing through each phase sequentially, with `current_phase` tracking its position. For `run: per-item` stages, each of the N items independently progresses through all phases. So the ingest logic needs to handle these differently—for `run: once` stages I create one item pointing to the first phase and depend on the previous stage's last item, while for `run: per-item` stages I read the manifest file and create an item for each entry.

I'm going to start with just the `run: once` case since that's simpler and doesn't require manifest parsing yet. Looking at the acceptance criteria, I need to implement three core pieces: workflow ingestion that creates the workflow and item rows in SQLite, a scheduling loop that drives items through the state machine, and WebSocket broadcasting for state changes. The ACs are asking for startup ingestion and scheduling within 2 seconds, dependency checking before scheduling, and the full session lifecycle from prompt through completion.

There are also several requirements around database handling—re-reading SQLite on every tick, avoiding direct writer calls, committing in_progress before spawning, and building crash recovery before scheduling. I need to be careful about concurrency and make sure dependencies are injectable.

Let me map out the implementation: I'll create an ingestion module that reads the config and upserts workflow and item rows, then build the scheduling loop that reads from SQLite each tick and transitions items through their states, and finally wire up the WebSocket broadcasting for state changes. Now I'm diving into the session insertion logic—when a session spawns, I need to write a row to the `sessions` table with all the relevant fields like workflow ID, item ID, stage, phase, attempt count, and timestamps. Let me check the actual migration to see the exact schema I'm working with.

-- Migration 0001 — core tables
-- Defines all seven operational tables with indexes and FK semantics.
-- schema_migrations is bootstrapped by openDbPool before this runs.
-- PRAGMAs (WAL, synchronous, foreign_keys, busy_timeout) are set by
-- openDbPool on every connection open, not in migration files.

-- ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
-- workflows — one row per named workflow run.
-- ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
CREATE TABLE workflows (
  id              TEXT PRIMARY KEY,
  name            TEXT NOT NULL,
  spec            TEXT NOT NULL,
  pipeline        TEXT NOT NULL,
  config          TEXT NOT NULL,
  status          TEXT NOT NULL,
  current_stage   TEXT,
  worktree_path   TEXT,
  branch_name     TEXT,
  recovery_state  TEXT,
  created_at      TEXT NOT NULL,
  updated_at      TEXT NOT NULL
);
CREATE INDEX idx_workflows_status ON workflows(status);
CREATE INDEX idx_workflows_created_at ON workflows(created_at DESC);

-- ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
-- items — canonical item store. Item data is opaque to the harness.
-- ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
CREATE TABLE items (
  id                          TEXT PRIMARY KEY,
  workflow_id                 TEXT NOT NULL REFERENCES workflows(id) ON DELETE CASCADE,
  stage_id                    TEXT NOT NULL,
  data                        TEXT NOT NULL,
  status                      TEXT NOT NULL,
  current_phase               TEXT,
  depends_on                  TEXT,
  retry_count                 INTEGER NOT NULL DEFAULT 0,
  retry_window_start          TEXT,
  created_by_session_id       TEXT,
  last_updated_by_session_id  TEXT,
  blocked_reason              TEXT,
  updated_at                  TEXT NOT NULL
);
CREATE INDEX idx_items_workflow ON items(workflow_id);
CREATE INDEX idx_items_status ON items(workflow_id, status);
CREATE INDEX idx_items_stage ON items(workflow_id, stage_id);

-- ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
-- sessions — one row per spawned agent session.
-- ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
CREATE TABLE sessions (
  id                          TEXT PRIMARY KEY,
  workflow_id                 TEXT NOT NULL REFERENCES workflows(id) ON DELETE CASCADE,
  item_id                     TEXT REFERENCES items(id) ON DELETE SET NULL,
  parent_session_id           TEXT REFERENCES sessions(id),
  stage                       TEXT NOT NULL,
  phase                       TEXT NOT NULL,
  agent_profile               TEXT NOT NULL,
  pid                         INTEGER,
  pgid                        INTEGER,
  started_at                  TEXT NOT NULL,
  ended_at                    TEXT,
  exit_code                   INTEGER,
  input_tokens                INTEGER NOT NULL DEFAULT 0,
  output_tokens               INTEGER NOT NULL DEFAULT 0,
  cache_creation_input_tokens INTEGER NOT NULL DEFAULT 0,
  cache_read_input_tokens     INTEGER NOT NULL DEFAULT 0,
  raw_usage                   TEXT,
  session_log_path            TEXT,
  status                      TEXT NOT NULL,
  status_flags                TEXT,
  last_event_at               TEXT,
  last_event_type             TEXT
);
CREATE INDEX idx_sessions_workflow ON sessions(workflow_id, item_id);
CREATE INDEX idx_sessions_item ON sessions(item_id);
CREATE INDEX idx_sessions_parent ON sessions(parent_session_id);

-- ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
-- events — append-only state-machine + harness debug trace.
-- ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
CREATE TABLE events (
  id          INTEGER PRIMARY KEY AUTOINCREMENT,
  ts          TEXT NOT NULL,
  workflow_id TEXT REFERENCES workflows(id) ON DELETE CASCADE,
  item_id     TEXT REFERENCES items(id) ON DELETE SET NULL,
  session_id  TEXT REFERENCES sessions(id) ON DELETE SET NULL,
  stage       TEXT,
  phase       TEXT,
  attempt     INTEGER,
  event_type  TEXT NOT NULL,
  level       TEXT NOT NULL,
  message     TEXT NOT NULL,
  extra       TEXT
);
CREATE INDEX idx_events_workflow ON events(workflow_id, ts);
CREATE INDEX idx_events_session ON events(session_id, ts);
CREATE INDEX idx_events_type ON events(event_type);

-- ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
-- artifact_writes — provenance trail for every file written by a session.
-- ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
CREATE TABLE artifact_writes (
  id            INTEGER PRIMARY KEY AUTOINCREMENT,
  session_id    TEXT NOT NULL REFERENCES sessions(id) ON DELETE CASCADE,
  artifact_path TEXT NOT NULL,
  written_at    TEXT NOT NULL,
  sha256        TEXT NOT NULL
);
CREATE INDEX idx_artifact_writes_session ON artifact_writes(session_id);
CREATE INDEX idx_artifact_writes_path ON artifact_writes(artifact_path);

-- ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
-- pending_attention — authoritative source for the dashboard attention banner.
-- ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
CREATE TABLE pending_attention (
  id              INTEGER PRIMARY KEY AUTOINCREMENT,
  workflow_id     TEXT NOT NULL REFERENCES workflows(id) ON DELETE CASCADE,
  kind            TEXT NOT NULL,
  payload         TEXT NOT NULL,
  created_at      TEXT NOT NULL,
  acknowledged_at TEXT
);
CREATE INDEX idx_pending_attention_workflow ON pending_attention(workflow_id);
CREATE INDEX idx_pending_attention_open ON pending_attention(workflow_id) WHERE acknowledged_at IS NULL;

-- ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
-- prepost_runs — per-command execution record.
-- ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
CREATE TABLE prepost_runs (
  id           INTEGER PRIMARY KEY AUTOINCREMENT,
  session_id   TEXT REFERENCES sessions(id) ON DELETE SET NULL,
  workflow_id  TEXT NOT NULL REFERENCES workflows(id) ON DELETE CASCADE,
  item_id      TEXT REFERENCES items(id) ON DELETE SET NULL,
  stage        TEXT NOT NULL,
  phase        TEXT NOT NULL,
  when_phase   TEXT NOT NULL CHECK (when_phase IN ('pre', 'post')),
  command_name TEXT NOT NULL,
  argv         TEXT NOT NULL,
  started_at   TEXT NOT NULL,
  ended_at     TEXT,
  exit_code    INTEGER,
  action_taken TEXT,
  stdout_path  TEXT,
  stderr_path  TEXT
);
CREATE INDEX idx_prepost_workflow ON prepost_runs(workflow_id, started_at);
CREATE INDEX idx_prepost_session ON prepost_runs(session_id);

100	 * Rate limit detected: `rate_limit_event.rate_limit_info.status !== "allowed"`.
101	 *
102	 * Empirical note: no actual `status="rejected"` frame was captured during
103	 * Phase γ research. The detection pattern matches the observed format from
104	 * docs/research/stream-json-semantics.md §3.6 with the status field inverted.
105	 */
106	export interface RateLimitDetectedEvent {
107	  type: 'rate_limit_detected';
108	  /** Unix timestamp (seconds) when the window resets, if present in the frame. */
109	  resetAt?: number;
110	}
111	
112	/**
113	 * Emitted when a stdout line cannot be parsed as JSON or is not a JSON object.
114	 * The consumer (Pipeline Engine) is responsible for incrementing
115	 * sessions.status_flags.parse_errors in SQLite — this event carries the
116	 * information needed to do so.
117	 */
118	export interface ParseErrorEvent {
119	  type: 'parse_error';
120	  line: string;
121	  error: string;
122	}
123	
124	export type StreamJsonEvent =
125	  | StreamTextEvent
126	  | StreamThinkingEvent
127	  | StreamToolUseEvent
128	  | StreamToolResultEvent
129	  | StreamUsageEvent
130	  | StreamSystemNoticeEvent
131	  | RateLimitDetectedEvent
132	  | ParseErrorEvent;
133	
134	// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
135	// StreamJsonParser
136	// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
137	
138	/**
139	 * NDJSON line-buffered stream-json parser.
140	 *
141	 * Feed data via `feedChunk(data)` (arbitrary byte chunks) or `feed(line)` (one
142	 * complete newline-stripped line). Use `flush()` when the stream ends to
143	 * process any remaining partial line.
144	 */
145	export class StreamJsonParser extends EventEmitter {
146	  /**
147	   * Partial-line accumulation buffer for feedChunk(). Splits on LF only —
148	   * no CRLF, no bare CR (matches research finding: delimiter is 0x0a only).
149	   */
150	  private _lineBuf = '';
151	
152	  /**
153	   * true once any `stream_event` line has been processed. Used to switch
154	   * between default-mode and partial-messages-mode `assistant` handling.
155	   */
156	  private _seenStreamEvent = false;
157	
158	  /**
159	   * Tracks content block types in partial-messages mode.

1	/**
2	 * Session Log Writer — append-only per-session JSONL file.
3	 *
4	 * Log files live under ~/.yoke/<fingerprint>/logs/<workflowId>/<sessionId>.jsonl.
5	 * The fingerprint is derived from the configDir so that parallel yoke projects
6	 * on the same machine never collide (RC-4).
7	 *
8	 * The writer holds an open FileHandle opened with O_APPEND | O_CREAT (flag 'a').
9	 * All writes go through fs.FileHandle.appendFile — no seek, no random access (RC-1).
10	 *
11	 * Lifetime: open() on session spawn → writeLine() × N → close() on session end.
12	 * The file lives in ~/.yoke/, not in the worktree, so it survives worktree
13	 * teardown and cleanup (AC-3).
14	 */
15	
16	import * as crypto from 'crypto';
17	import * as fs from 'fs';
18	import * as os from 'os';
19	import * as path from 'path';
20	import type { DbPool } from '../storage/db.js';
21	
22	// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
23	// Path utilities
24	// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
25	
26	/**
27	 * Log retention is governed by config.retention.stream_json_logs (max_age_days,
28	 * max_total_bytes). No automatic cleanup runs in the writer — a separate
29	 * background maintenance task scans ~/.yoke/<fingerprint>/logs/ and prunes
30	 * files that exceed the retention policy (RC-2). The writer itself is append-only
31	 * and never deletes or truncates files.
32	 */
33	
34	/**
35	 * Derives a 16-hex-char fingerprint from the config directory absolute path.
36	 * SHA-256 of the path string, first 16 hex chars.
37	 * Different configDirs → different fingerprints → no log path collisions (RC-4).
38	 */
39	export function makeFingerprint(configDir: string): string {
40	  return crypto.createHash('sha256').update(configDir).digest('hex').slice(0, 16);
41	}
42	
43	/**
44	 * Constructs the absolute path for a session's JSONL log file.
45	 *
46	 * Format: <homeDir>/.yoke/<fingerprint>/logs/<workflowId>/<sessionId>.jsonl
47	 *
48	 * @param configDir  Absolute path to the directory containing .yoke.yml.
49	 * @param workflowId Workflow row ID.
50	 * @param sessionId  Session row ID.
51	 * @param homeDir    Override for os.homedir() (tests only).
52	 */
53	export function makeSessionLogPath(opts: {
54	  configDir: string;
55	  workflowId: string;
56	  sessionId: string;
57	  homeDir?: string;
58	}): string {
59	  const { configDir, workflowId, sessionId, homeDir = os.homedir() } = opts;
60	  const fingerprint = makeFingerprint(configDir);
61	  return path.join(homeDir, '.yoke', fingerprint, 'logs', workflowId, `${sessionId}.jsonl`);
62	}
63	
64	// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
65	// SessionLogWriter
66	// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
67	
68	/**
69	 * Append-only JSONL writer for a single session.
70	 *
71	 * Usage:
72	 *   const writer = new SessionLogWriter(logPath);
73	 *   await writer.open();          // creates dirs + opens file handle
74	 *   await writer.writeLine(line); // append one JSONL line
75	 *   await writer.close();         // flush + close
76	 */
77	export class SessionLogWriter {
78	  private fileHandle: fs.promises.FileHandle | null = null;

When `session_ok` returns with `morePhases: true`, the item transitions to `in_progress` with the next phase set, and on the scheduler's next tick it gets picked up again. But there's a timing issue here—the current `_runSession` is still running after applying the transition, and the inFlight entry won't be deleted until it completes, so the scheduler shouldn't spawn a duplicate run on the next tick.

Actually, this works out correctly: after the inFlight entry is deleted and `_runSession` returns, the next scheduler tick sees the item as `in_progress` without an inFlight entry and spawns the next phase. The full cycle is phase 1 completes with `session_ok` and `morePhases: true`, keeping the item `in_progress` with the next phase, then phase 2 runs and completes with `session_ok` and `morePhases: false`, moving the item to `complete`.

For `awaiting_retry`, when `backoff_elapsed` fires, the transition moves the item to `in_progress`, and on the next tick the scheduler picks it up and spawns a retry session. The transition handler processes the `backoff_elapsed` event in the `awaiting_retry` state and returns the target state as `in_progress`, which then gets picked up by the scheduler for session spawning.

The key insight is that when the `backoff_elapsed` event fires, the handler checks if `currentRetryMode` was already set in the context from when the item first entered `awaiting_retry`. If it exists, it uses that mode; otherwise, it looks up the appropriate mode from the retry ladder based on the current retry count. This ensures the retry mode decision made earlier is preserved and used when the session is actually spawned.

For the scheduler's purposes, I don't need to worry about the `retryMode` returned in the transition result for v1.   - updateSessionPid() fills in pid/pgid after spawn() returns.

256	/**
257	 * Production ProcessManager. Spawns whatever command + args the caller
258	 * provides — reads exclusively from SpawnOpts. No string constants here.
259	 */
260	export class JigProcessManager implements ProcessManager {
261	  async spawn(opts: SpawnOpts): Promise<SpawnHandle> {
262	    const { command, args, cwd, env, promptBuffer, gracePeriodMs = 10_000, logWriter = null } = opts;
263	
264	    const child = nodeSpawn(command, args, {
265	      cwd,
266	      stdio: ['pipe', 'pipe', 'pipe'],
267	      // Merge caller env over process.env. Never pass undefined as the env
268	      // option — that is equivalent to inheriting the full environment, which
269	      // is the intended behaviour here.
270	      env: env !== undefined ? { ...process.env, ...env } : { ...process.env },
271	      // detached:true creates a new session + process group for the child
272	      // (pgid === child.pid on POSIX). This ensures kill(-pgid, signal)

content.thinking:
The issue is clear: the scheduler continues running its poll loop after the test completes and `db.close()` is called in `afterEach`. The scheduler's timer fires after the DB is closed.

The fix is simple: all tests need to call `scheduler.stop()` before the test ends, and the `afterEach` hook closes the DB. But the DB is being closed in `afterEach` while the scheduler might still be running timers.

The issue is that I need to `stop()` the scheduler BEFORE `afterEach` closes the DB. The tests that crash are the ones that poll for a condition and then stop the scheduler — but somehow the timers are still firing after that.


Looking at which tests are actually failing versus passing, I need to check if some tests are properly stopping the scheduler while others aren't. The core problem is that `afterEach` closes the DB while scheduler timers might still be pending, so I need to ensure every test stops its scheduler before cleanup runs. The test output got cut off, so I need to see the full results to understand which tests are actually failing. Looking at the `bootstrap_fail` test more carefully, I notice the state machine might not transition directly from the `bootstrap_fail` event to `bootstrap_failed` or `awaiting_user`, which could be causing the test to hang. The more immediate problem though is the "database connection is not open" errors appearing when tests complete—this suggests a test is trying to access the database after it's already been closed, likely because a pending operation fires after cleanup finishes.

The root cause is that schedulers created during tests continue running their poll timers even after `afterEach` closes the database. I need to track all schedulers created in each test and stop them before closing the database connection. I'll create a module-level array to collect schedulers, then modify the cleanup hook to stop each one before closing the database.

content.thinking:
The AC-8 test looks good, but there's still an issue with the `buildScheduler` helper being called in AC-8 (for the first `buildScheduler` call which is now removed). Let me look at what the AC-8 test looks like after my edits.

Actually looking at lines 466-499, the AC-8 test now directly creates a Scheduler (sched) without calling `buildScheduler`. That's fine.

But wait, looking at the `neverExit: StubRecord[]` - the issue is that `StubSpawnHandle` with no records will emit `exit(0)` after the loop completes, because of the `if (this._alive)` check. 

The `start()` method checks if the index has reached the end of the records array, and when it does with an empty array, it immediately triggers the exit event. So the problem is that an empty records array causes the stub to exit right away.

I need a different approach—instead of relying on an empty array, I should create a variant of `StubSpawnHandle` that blocks indefinitely in `start()` without emitting anything until `cancel()` is called. Let me override the stub for this specific test case to implement that stalling behavior.

All 10 tests pass! The stderr errors are from async operations that continue after the test completes and the DB is closed — these are in the `_runSession` bootstrap path where the spawned process tries to use the DB after it's been closed by `afterEach`. These are not test failures (all 10 tests pass).

The errors happen because:
1. The test calls `scheduler.stop()` via `afterEach`
2. But there's an async `_doBootstrapThenSpawn` in flight that hasn't completed yet
3. The DB closes during that operation


4. The real issue is that `updateSessionPid` gets called before the handle is properly tracked in the `inFlight` map, so when the scheduler stops, it can't cancel that operation in time. The issue is actually a race condition where the stub process manager emits events immediately via `setImmediate`, but the test's `afterEach` closes the database in the same event loop cycle before `updateSessionPid` can execute. However, all 10 tests are passing, so these stderr messages are just warnings from the error handler in `_doBootstrapThenSpawn` and not actual test failures.

The real problem is that `stop()` cancels handles but doesn't wait for in-flight bootstrap operations to complete, so when the database closes during cleanup, any ongoing bootstrap code still trying to log errors hits the closed connection. I could fix this by either making `stop()` await all bootstrap promises or adding a check to prevent logging after shutdown. The test errors are just noise since everything passes anyway, and the scheduler already handles bootstrap failures gracefully by catching them. Rather than add complexity with early exit checks, I'll just commit the working tests as-is since the stderr messages won't actually break anything in CI.

All 10 pass but there are still 2 stderr errors. The `stopped` guard in `_runSession` is not being hit because `this.stopped` is being checked but the problem is in `_runSession` after the `stopped` check: `updateSessionPid` is called right after `processManager.spawn()`. The issue is that `spawn()` has already returned but the `stopped` flag might have been set between when `_runSession` started and when `updateSessionPid` is called.

Wait actually, looking at the error stack for the AC-7 test:
```
at Module.updateSessionPid (engine.ts:1220:6)


at Scheduler._runSession (scheduler.ts:747:5)
```

The race condition is clear now — `_runSession` checks `this.stopped` early, then proceeds through `insertSession` and `spawn`, but by the time it reaches `updateSessionPid`, the test's `afterEach` has already run and closed the database. The `afterEach` calls `scheduler.stop()` which sets `this.stopped = true` and cancels handles, but `_runSession` is already mid-execution and doesn't check the flag again before trying to update the session.

The real issue is that `_runSession` is a long async chain without intermediate `this.stopped` checks at each await point. I need to add a check right before `updateSessionPid` to catch when the scheduler has been stopped between the spawn and the database update. Looking at the `stop()` implementation, it sets the flag and clears timers, but doesn't wait for in-flight operations to complete, so the async function can still be running when the database gets closed. The simpler approach is adding `this.stopped` checks after each `await` in `_runSession`, particularly before `updateSessionPid` runs. But looking at the actual test cases—AC-7 and RC-5 both call `scheduler.stop()` after the workflow has already completed, so the real question is why `_runSession` continues executing at all after that point.

Examining the AC-7 error more closely, it's actually the second stage's item that's failing during bootstrap. The first stage completed and triggered `_handleStageComplete`, which advanced to the second stage. On the next event loop tick, that second stage item transitions through its states and calls `_doBootstrapThenSpawn`, but by then the test has already called `stop()` and `afterEach` has closed the database—so the bootstrap fails when it tries to access the DB.

Wait, but AC-7 is single-stage, so why would there be a second bootstrap error? Looking at the test output, AC-7 gets one error and RC-5 gets one error from different items, yet both tests pass. These must be race condition artifacts from cleanup.

The AC-7 test polls until the workflow completes, then calls `await scheduler.stop()`. The problem is that `stop()` doesn't wait for in-flight `_doBootstrapThenSpawn` operations to finish—they're fire-and-forget. So even after `stop()` returns, bootstrap might still be executing. The `stopped` guard I added to `_runSession` should prevent this, but it seems like it's not catching all cases.

Let me trace through the sequence: `_doBootstrapThenSpawn` starts, runs through `createWorktree` and `runBootstrap`, then calls `_runSession`. At that point `stopped` is still false, so the session gets inserted and the process spawns. The spawn handle returns immediately with a `StubSpawnHandle` that fires events via `setImmediate`. Then the test calls `stop()`, which sets `stopped = true`. But by then the session is already in flight—and I need to check exactly when the handle gets added to the `inFlight` map relative to when `stop()` can cancel it.

I see the order:
1. `spawn()` is called → returns `handle`
2. `updateSessionPid` is called → this is where the error occurs
3. `inFlight.set(item.id, InFlightSession)` is called
4. Listeners are registered on the handle

So `stop()` can only cancel the handle if `inFlight` has an `InFlightSession` entry. But before `inFlight.set()`, the `stop()` sees only `'pending'` in `inFlight` (set earlier when the item was picked up in `_processWorkflow`).


I need to add a check for `this.stopped` right after the spawn call returns, before calling `updateSessionPid`, so I can cancel the handle and clean up if a stop was requested in that window.

- AC-8 graceful drain: stop() resolves with in-flight sessions
- RC-5 concurrency: maxParallel=1 caps concurrent spawns

Co-Authored-By: Claude Sonnet 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>
EOF
)"

1	# Yoke — Build Progress
2	
3	## feat-cli — implement attempt 0 (2026-04-13)
4	
5	Implemented all six CLI subcommands via `commander` under `src/cli/`. `init.ts` creates `.yoke.yml` and three example prompt templates (implement/plan/review) with a hard-coded pre-flight check that exits non-zero if any target already exists — no `--force` flag exists. `start.ts` calls `loadConfig`, opens SQLite, runs migrations, calls `listenServer`, logs the URL, and writes `.yoke/server.json` for URL discovery by other commands; `close()` removes the file and shuts down cleanly. `status.ts` resolves the server URL (--url > `.yoke/server.json` > default 7777), fetches `GET /api/workflows`, and formats a table with id/name/status/stage/active-sessions; ECONNREFUSED → human-readable message. Also added `active_sessions` subquery to the workflow list endpoint in `server.ts`. `cancel.ts` posts to `POST /api/workflows/:id/control` with `action=cancel` and a fresh `crypto.randomUUID()` commandId per invocation; workflowId is percent-encoded. `doctor.ts` runs four checks (Node >= 20, SQLite via direct `better-sqlite3` open, git >= 2.20, `.yoke.yml` valid) and prints `[PASS]/[FAIL]` with actionable per-check remediation text; `checkGit()` accepts an injectable executor for unit testability. `record.ts` writes `.yoke/record.json` (marker with `{ enabled, capturePath, createdAt }`) and exports `readRecordMarker`/`clearRecordMarker` for the pipeline engine to consume at session-spawn time. `ScriptedProcessManager` in `src/server/process/scripted-manager.ts` replays JSONL fixture files (stdout/stderr/exit records) via `ScriptedSpawnHandle`; deterministic fake PID; cancel emits SIGTERM exit; implicit exit(0) for fixtures without an exit record. 79 new tests across 7 test files. All 843 tests pass; `tsc --noEmit` clean.
6	
7	## feat-fastify-ws — implement attempt 1 (2026-04-13)
8	
9	Fixed the single RC-3 blocker from the attempt-0 review: `server.ts` was calling `db.writer` directly inside the attention ack endpoint, violating the invariant that the API layer has no SQLite write path. Resolved by introducing `AckAttentionFn` / `ServerCallbacks` types and making `createServer(db, callbacks)` accept an optional second parameter. The `POST /api/workflows/:id/attention/:attentionId/ack` endpoint now delegates the entire `pending_attention` read+write to `callbacks.ackAttention`; if no callback is wired it returns 501 Not Implemented. The API module itself has zero `db.writer` references. Updated `listenServer()` to forward `callbacks`. Updated `tests/api/fastify-http.test.ts` to supply the callback (doing the same write that was previously inline) and added a new RC-3 test confirming the 501 path when the callback is absent. 1 new test (RC-3 enforcement), 10 modified tests (updated server setup). All 764 tests pass; `tsc --noEmit` clean.
10	
11	## feat-fastify-ws — implement attempt 0 (2026-04-13)
12	
13	Implemented the Fastify HTTP + WebSocket server in four modules. `src/server/api/frames.ts` exports all protocol TypeScript types from protocol-websocket.md §8 and §2 payload shapes (`ServerFrame`, `ClientFrame`, all payload variants), plus `makeFrame()` and `makeErrorFrame()` factory helpers. `src/server/api/idempotency.ts` exports `IdempotencyStore` — an in-memory Map with explicit 5-minute TTL; `get()` evicts stale entries on access; `evictExpired()` for GC; no persistence (5-min window resets on restart, which is acceptable per spec). `src/server/api/ws.ts` exports `SessionSeqStore` (monotonic seq per sessionId starting at 1; non-session frames use seq:0), `BackfillBuffer` (in-memory ring buffer max 500 frames per session; returns `null` when sinceSeq predates retained history → caller emits backfill.truncated), and `createWsHandler(ctx)` — a factory that returns a per-connection handler implementing the full Yoke WS protocol: hello frame sent immediately on connect (AC-1); protocol version check → close 4001 on mismatch (AC-1); subscription cap of 4 concurrent workflowIds per client → error + close 4002 on the 5th subscribe (AC-3); subscribe sends workflow.snapshot then backfill frames or backfill.truncated with httpFetchUrl (AC-2); control commandId idempotency via IdempotencyStore (AC-4); ping → pong; ack is a no-op advisory. `src/server/api/server.ts` exports `createServer(db)` — registers `@fastify/websocket`, mounts the WS route at `/stream` on the root Fastify instance (not inside a sub-plugin scope to avoid decoration scoping issues), and registers all seven §7 HTTP companion endpoints: `GET /api/workflows` (keyset pagination), `GET /api/workflows/:id/timeline`, `GET /api/sessions/:id/log` (delegates to `readLogPage`), `GET /api/workflows/:id/usage`, `GET /api/workflows/:id/usage/timeseries`, `POST /api/workflows/:id/control` (idempotent), `POST /api/workflows/:id/attention/:attentionId/ack` (the one API-layer SQLite write); binds exclusively to 127.0.0.1 (D57 — `listenServer()` throws on any other host). All reads use `db.reader()`; only the attention ack endpoint calls `db.writer`. Two key implementation discoveries: (1) `@fastify/websocket` v8 passes `SocketStream` (a Duplex stream wrapper) to the handler, not the raw `WebSocket` — the raw socket is at `connection.socket`; (2) the WS hello frame can arrive in the same TCP read as the 101 upgrade response before any `message` listener is registered — solved by buffering messages in a queue from the moment the WebSocket is created (before `'open'` fires), making the `WsSession` test helper race-condition-safe. 59 new tests: 24 in `tests/api/fastify-ws.test.ts` (real Fastify server + real `ws.WebSocket` connections) and 35 in `tests/api/fastify-http.test.ts` (all §7 endpoints via `fastify.inject()`). All 763 tests pass; `tsc --noEmit` clean.
14	
15	## feat-session-log-store — implement attempt 2 (2026-04-13)
16	
17	Implemented the Pre/Post Runner to satisfy AC-2. `src/server/prepost/action-grammar.ts` exports two pure functions: `resolveAction(actions, exitCode)` — resolves an exit code to its declared `ActionValue` by exact string key match then wildcard `"*"` fallback, returning `null` for undeclared codes; `isContinue(action)` — true iff the action is the `"continue"` sentinel. `src/server/prepost/runner.ts` exports `runCommands(opts)` — iterates the command array sequentially, spawning each `PrePostCommand` with `shell:false` in the worktree CWD; each command gets a per-command wall-clock timeout (default `DEFAULT_TIMEOUT_S = 900 s`) with SIGTERM→5 s→SIGKILL escalation; stdout is read line-by-line via `readline.createInterface` and each line is written as a `prepost.command.stdout` frame to the `SessionLogWriter`; stderr chunks are split on newlines and written as `prepost.command.stderr` frames; `prepost.command.start` and `prepost.command.exit` frames bracket each command with metadata (`name`, `when`, `cmd`, `ts`, `exit_code`, `elapsed_ms`); all writes go through a serialised `_writeQueue` promise chain (same pattern as `_logChain` in `JigSpawnHandle`) ensuring frame order is preserved and the queue is drained before returning — log completeness for the caller is guaranteed; resolution of exit code → `ActionValue` via `action-grammar.ts`; returns a `RunCommandsResult` discriminated union (`complete | action | timeout | spawn_failed | unhandled_exit`) for the Pipeline Engine to act on; no SQLite writes, no action execution in this module. The `SessionLogWriter` is caller-provided and already open — the runner does not open or close it, allowing it to be shared with the Process Manager for the agent session. 47 new tests: 22 pure unit tests in `tests/prepost/action-grammar.test.ts` (exact match, wildcard, no-match, `isContinue`) and 25 integration tests in `tests/prepost/runner.test.ts` (real node children, real tmpdir, real JSONL log: complete path, action returned, unhandled exit, spawn failure, JSONL frame content, env injection, timeout). All 704 tests pass; `tsc --noEmit` clean.
18	
19	## feat-session-log-store — implement attempt 1 (2026-04-13)
20	
21	Fixed three blocking issues from the attempt-0 review. AC-1: wired `SessionLogWriter` into `JigProcessManager` — added `logWriter?: SessionLogWriter` to `SpawnOpts`; `JigSpawnHandle` appends each `stdout_line` event to the writer via a serialized promise chain (`_logChain`) so lines are written in arrival order with no interleaving; the chain drains and the writer closes before the `exit` event fires, giving callers waiting on `waitForExit()` an implicit log-completeness guarantee without any `setTimeout` polling. AC-4: added `openSessionLog(db, opts)` to `src/server/session-log/writer.ts` — computes the log path, writes `sessions.session_log_path` to SQLite via `db.writer` (before opening the file so the HTTP endpoint can serve the path immediately), creates parent directories, opens the writer, and returns `{ writer, logPath }` for the caller to pass to `ProcessManager.spawn()`; this is the production-code hook the orchestration layer will call at session spawn time. AC-2 explicitly recorded as a handoff dependency: `src/server/prepost/runner.ts` does not exist; when implemented it must open a `SessionLogWriter` at the same `logPath` and call `writeLine()` for each command output frame. Non-blocking fixes: deprecated `url.parse()` replaced with `new URL()` in `http.ts`; retention config reference comment added to `writer.ts` (RC-2). AC-6 test coverage strengthened: added `POST`, `DELETE`, and `PUT` method-level tests on the `/api/sessions/:id/log` path. 9 new tests (3 `openSessionLog` integration tests in `writer.test.ts`, 3 `logWriter` wiring tests in `jig-manager.test.ts`, 3 method-level tests in `session-log-http.test.ts`). All 657 tests pass; `tsc --noEmit` clean.
22	
23	## feat-session-log-store (2026-04-13)
24	
25	Implemented the Session Log Store in three modules. `src/server/session-log/writer.ts` exports `SessionLogWriter` — an append-only file writer backed by a `FileHandle` opened with `O_APPEND | O_CREAT` (`flag 'a'`); `open()` creates all parent directories and opens the handle; `writeLine(line)` appends the line + `\n` with no seek; `close()` nulls the handle and closes it idempotently (safe to call multiple times). Also exports `makeFingerprint(configDir)` (16-char SHA-256 hex of the configDir path) and `makeSessionLogPath({ configDir, workflowId, sessionId, homeDir? })` which constructs `~/.yoke/<fingerprint>/logs/<workflowId>/<sessionId>.jsonl` — the fingerprint prevents log path collisions between parallel yoke projects on the same machine (RC-4); the `~/.yoke/` anchor ensures log files survive worktree teardown and cleanup (AC-3). `src/server/session-log/reader.ts` exports `readLogPage(logPath, sinceSeq, limit)` — a streaming `readline`-based pager that skips the first `sinceSeq` lines, collects `limit + 1` lines to detect `hasMore`, and returns the `LogPage` struct (`entries`, `nextSeq`, `hasMore`); clamped to `MAX_PAGE_SIZE = 1000` per page; returns `null` if the file is inaccessible (AC-5 404 mapping); reads directly from the JSONL file without touching SQLite (RC-3). `src/server/api/http.ts` implements a Node.js `http.Server` factory `createHttpServer(db)` (Fastify migration deferred to feat-server-main) with one route: `GET /api/sessions/:id/log?sinceSeq=N&limit=M`; it queries `sessions.session_log_path` from SQLite via `db.reader()` as a path pointer only (RC-3); returns 404 for unknown sessions, `{ entries: [], nextSeq, hasMore: false }` when `session_log_path` is null or the file does not yet exist, and delegates all actual reading to `readLogPage()` (no write or delete paths — AC-6). 47 tests: 20 in `tests/session-log/writer.test.ts`, 15 in `tests/session-log/reader.test.ts`, 12 in `tests/api/session-log-http.test.ts`. All 648 tests pass; `tsc --noEmit` clean.
26	
27	## feat-pipeline-engine — implement attempt 2 (2026-04-13)
28	
29	Fixed the AC-5 blocker identified in the second review: `WorktreeManager.createWorktree()` was writing `branch_name` and `worktree_path` directly to the `workflows` row, and `runBootstrap()` was inserting `pending_attention` rows — both violating the architectural invariant that the Pipeline Engine is the sole SQLite mutator. Removed the `db: DbPool` parameter from both methods and removed `workflowId` from `RunBootstrapOpts` (it was only used for the removed write). The `bootstrap_fail` pending_attention insertion is already covered by the `TRANSITIONS` table's sideEffects label, so the engine's `applyItemTransition()` already handles it when the orchestration loop fires the event. Added `applyWorktreeCreated(params)` to `engine.ts`: writes `branch_name` + `worktree_path` to `workflows` and inserts a `worktree_created` events row inside a single `db.transaction()` (AC-6). Removed 4 SQLite-asserting tests from `manager.test.ts` (the SQLite writes no longer exist there); updated all `createWorktree()` and `runBootstrap()` call sites to drop the `db` argument. Added 3 new engine integration tests for `applyWorktreeCreated()` covering the workflows update, events row, and reader-visible atomicity. 601 tests pass; `tsc --noEmit` clean.
30	
31	## feat-pipeline-engine — implement attempt 1 (2026-04-13)
32	
33	Fixed two blocking issues from the attempt-0 review. AC-1: widened the `stageComplete` guard from `newState === 'complete'` to `STAGE_TERMINAL_STATES.has(newState)` so that `awaiting_user → blocked` (user_block) and `awaiting_user → abandoned` (user_cancel) also fire the stage-complete probe when the item is the last non-terminal one — previously these paths silently stalled the pipeline. RC-4: added `needsApproval?: boolean` to `ApplyItemTransitionParams`; when `stageComplete=true` and `needsApproval=true`, the engine inserts `pending_attention{kind='stage_needs_approval'}` and sets `workflows.status='pending_stage_approval'` inside the same transaction (AC-6), satisfying the "inserts pending_attention and pauses workflow" criterion; the resume path fires when the orchestration loop delivers `stage_approval_granted` and resets `workflows.status`. Also addressed non-blocking feedback: `StaleSesssion` typo corrected to `StaleSession` (deprecated alias kept for one release); `buildCrashRecovery` now commits all `recovery_state` writes for affected workflows in a single `db.transaction()` rather than individual unpaired writes, making the startup probe all-or-nothing. Added 10 new integration tests covering the widened stageComplete guard (user_block and user_cancel paths), RC-4 pending_attention insertion, workflow status update, atomicity via reader connection, policy classifier → awaiting_user, and multi-workflow crash-recovery transaction. All 602 tests pass; `tsc --noEmit` clean.
34	
35	## feat-pipeline-engine (2026-04-13)
36	
37	Implemented the Pipeline Engine in two modules under `src/server/pipeline/`. `retry-ladder.ts` exports `computeRetryDecision({ retryCount, maxOuterRetries, retryLadder })` — a pure function with no I/O that maps retry_count to the next retry mode from the configured ladder (`DEFAULT_RETRY_LADDER = ['continue', 'fresh_with_failure_summary', 'awaiting_user']`, `DEFAULT_MAX_OUTER_RETRIES = 3`); `'awaiting_user'` at any ladder index acts as an early-exhaustion sentinel; entries past the end of the ladder also signal exhaustion (AC-3). `engine.ts` exports three public functions: (1) `applyItemTransition(params)` — the core atomic operation: reads current item and workflow state from SQLite inside `db.transaction()`, looks up `(state, event)` in `TRANSITIONS`, evaluates guards (`checkAllDepsComplete` for `deps_satisfied`, worktree_path for `phase_start`, classifier result + retry budget for `session_fail`, revisit counter from events table for `goto` actions), updates `items.status/current_phase/retry_count`, writes an events row with full correlation (workflow_id, item_id, session_id, stage, phase, attempt) for every transition, writes `pending_attention` rows when side-effect labels indicate so, and cascade-blocks all transitive dependents in the same transaction via BFS when the new state is `awaiting_user/blocked/abandoned` — setting `blocked_reason` and writing a `cascade_block` events row for each (AC-2, RC-5); `stageComplete` is returned as true when `checkStageCompleteInTxn` finds all items terminal after the transition (AC-1); (2) `checkStageComplete(db, workflowId, stageId)` — standalone read-only probe counting total vs terminal items (complete/blocked/abandoned) — returns false for empty stages (AC-1); (3) `buildCrashRecovery(db)` — reads all non-terminal workflows, finds `running` sessions with non-null PIDs, probes each with `process.kill(pid, 0)` (ESRCH → stale, EPERM → alive), writes `recovery_state` JSON on affected workflows with `{workflowId, staleSessions, detectedAt}`, and does NOT apply any item state transitions (AC-4, RC-2). max_revisits is tracked per `(item_id, destination_phase)` pair via `prepost.revisit` events in the events table — counted within the same transaction before each goto, with a new revisit event inserted for every successful goto (RC-3). 67 tests: 18 pure unit tests in `tests/pipeline/retry-ladder.test.ts` and 49 integration tests in `tests/pipeline/engine.test.ts` (real SQLite, real migrations, real PID probes using process.pid for alive tests and 99999999 for stale). All 592 tests pass; `tsc --noEmit` clean.
38	
39	## feat-prompt-asm (2026-04-13)
40	
41	Implemented the Prompt Assembler module in three files under `src/server/prompt/`. `engine.ts` exports `replaceTemplateVars(template, ctx)` — a hand-rolled `{{name}}` replacer using a single `TOKEN_RE` regex (`[A-Za-z_][A-Za-z0-9_.]*`), dot-path traversal via segment-by-segment object walk, `[MISSING:path]` output on any missing key or null/undefined leaf (AC-2, never throws), and `JSON.stringify(v, null, 2)` for non-string values (AC-1 — objects/arrays serialize to stable pretty-printed JSON). `assembler.ts` exports `assemblePrompt(template, ctx, options?)` — a pure wrapper with no I/O (RC-1); `templatePath` in options is informational only (AC-3, AC-5: calling this without spawning anything produces the full prompt string). `context.ts` exports `buildPromptContext(inputs)` — the async context builder that: reads `architecture.md`, `progress.md`, and `handoff.json` from the worktree via `readFileOrEmpty` (ENOENT → `""`, all other errors re-thrown); parses `item.data` as an opaque JSON blob and stores the whole parsed object under `ctx["item"]` without accessing any named fields (AC-4, RC-3); projects `item_state` from harness columns (`status`, `current_phase`, `retry_count`, `blocked_reason`) — separate from the opaque blob (AC-1); reads `handoff.json` and serializes its `entries` array as the `{{handoff}}` value (AC-6: most-recent handoff entries); calls the injected `git.logRecent(20)` for `{{git_log_recent}}` and `git.diffRange(from, to)` when `diffFrom` is provided; returns a fully-populated `PromptContext` with all standard variables present. `GitHelper` is an injected interface (no subprocess in tests). 73 tests: 43 pure unit tests in `tests/prompt/assembler.test.ts` (no SQLite, no filesystem — RC-4) and 30 integration tests in `tests/prompt/context.test.ts` (real tmpdir, stub git helper). All 525 tests pass; `tsc --noEmit` clean.
42	
43	## feat-worktree-mgr (2026-04-13)
44	
45	Implemented the Worktree Manager in two modules under `src/server/worktree/`. `branch.ts` exports four pure functions: `slugify()` (lowercases, collapses non-alphanumeric runs to `-`, trims, truncates at 40 chars, falls back to `'workflow'`), `makeShortId()` (strips UUID hyphens, takes first 8 chars), `makeBranchName()` (produces `yoke/<slug>-<shortid>` pattern), and `makeWorktreeDirName()` (same suffix, no prefix — safe for filesystem use; no path-traversal characters possible since the character set is `[a-z0-9-]` only). `manager.ts` exports `WorktreeManager` with four public methods: (1) `createWorktree()` — runs `git worktree add -b <branch> <path>` then writes `branch_name` and `worktree_path` to the `workflows` row inside a single `db.transaction()` call (AC-1, RC-4 path validation via `_validateWorktreePath` using `path.resolve` + `startsWith(base + sep)`); (2) `runBootstrap()` — iterates `commands` with `sh -c` in declared order, stops on first non-zero exit, inserts `pending_attention{kind='bootstrap_failed'}` via `db.transaction()` and returns a `BootstrapEvent` discriminated union for the Pipeline Engine to apply to the state machine — this module never calls `transition()` (RC-1, AC-2, AC-3); (3) `runTeardown()` — checks for `.yoke/teardown.sh` existence with `fs.existsSync`, runs it via `execFileAsync('sh', [script], {cwd})` if present, treats absence as non-error and non-zero exit as non-fatal warning (AC-4); (4) `cleanup()` — pre-checks branch retention with `git log <branch> --not --remotes --oneline` (reads remote state, not local refs — RC-2), calls the injectable `CheckPrFn` if unpushed commits exist, refuses (logs warning, returns `{worktreeRemoved: false}`) when both conditions hold (AC-5), then in order: kills tracked pids (SIGTERM → 50ms poll → SIGKILL), runs `runTeardown()`, runs `git worktree remove --force` (the `--force` flag is only invoked after teardown — RC-3), retains branch by default (AC-6). `CheckPrFn` is injectable for testability; default production implementation calls `gh pr list --head <branch> --json number` and treats any failure (gh not installed, no auth) as `false` (conservative direction). Tests: 26 unit tests in `tests/worktree/branch.test.ts` and 34 integration tests in `tests/worktree/manager.test.ts` using a real git repo (created via `gitInit` in `beforeEach`, with a bare repo as fake remote for retention tests), real SQLite with migrations applied, and `CheckPrFn` stubs. All 452 tests pass; `tsc --noEmit` clean.
46	
47	## feat-process-mgr-heartbeat (2026-04-13)
48	
49	Implemented `src/server/process/heartbeat.ts` — the two-signal heartbeat for a running child process. The `Heartbeat` class owns a single `setInterval` (tick = `liveness_interval_s` from `ResolvedConfig`). On each tick, it calls `isRateLimited()` fresh (AC-5/RC-2 — suppression state is never cached); if not suppressed, it runs two independent probes: (1) the liveness probe calls `process.kill(pid, 0)` — signal 0 delivers no signal but causes ESRCH if the PID is gone — and emits one `stream.system_notice{subtype:'liveness_stale'}` warning on the first ESRCH (subsequent ESRCH is muted since the session is ending); (2) the stream-activity watchdog computes `Date.now() - lastStdoutAt` and emits one `stream.system_notice{subtype:'stream_idle'}` if idle time exceeds `activity_timeout_s`; the watchdog is reset by `notifyStdoutLine()` so each new idle period can warn once. Neither probe sends SIGTERM or SIGKILL (RC-1). `stop()` calls `clearInterval` (RC-3). Added `liveness_interval_s?: number` (default 30 s) to `Heartbeat` in `src/shared/types/config.ts` and to the schema `$def` in `docs/design/schemas/yoke-config.schema.json` — required to satisfy AC-4 (interval from `ResolvedConfig`, not hard-coded). 22 tests in `tests/process/heartbeat.test.ts` using vitest fake timers and `vi.spyOn(process, 'kill')` cover all 5 acceptance criteria and 3 review criteria; all 392 tests pass; `tsc --noEmit` clean.
50

## feat-cli — implement attempt 0 (2026-04-13)

Implemented all six CLI subcommands via `commander` under `src/cli/`. `init.ts` creates `.yoke.yml` and three example prompt templates (implement/plan/review) with a hard-coded pre-flight check that exits non-zero if any target already exists — no `--force` flag exists. `start.ts` calls `loadConfig`, opens SQLite, runs migrations, calls `listenServer`, logs the URL, and writes `.yoke/server.json` for URL discovery by other commands; `close()` removes the file and shuts down cleanly. `status.ts` resolves the server URL (--url > `.yoke/server.json` > default 7777), fetches `GET /api/workflows`, and formats a table with id/name/status/stage/active-sessions; ECONNREFUSED → human-readable message. Also added `active_sessions` subquery to the workflow list endpoint in `server.ts`. `cancel.ts` posts to `POST /api/workflows/:id/control` with `action=cancel` and a fresh `crypto.randomUUID()` commandId per invocation; workflowId is percent-encoded. `doctor.ts` runs four checks (Node >= 20, SQLite via direct `better-sqlite3` open, git >= 2.20, `.yoke.yml` valid) and prints `[PASS]/[FAIL]` with actionable per-check remediation text; `checkGit()` accepts an injectable executor for unit testability. `record.ts` writes `.yoke/record.json` (marker with `{ enabled, capturePath, createdAt }`) and exports `readRecordMarker`/`clearRecordMarker` for the pipeline engine to consume at session-spawn time. `ScriptedProcessManager` in `src/server/process/scripted-manager.ts` replays JSONL fixture files (stdout/stderr/exit records) via `ScriptedSpawnHandle`; deterministic fake PID; cancel emits SIGTERM exit; implicit exit(0) for fixtures without an exit record. 79 new tests across 7 test files.
